prompt: The environmental impact of fashion industry is
baseline_text:
The environmental impact of fashion industry is now much more prominent in our daily lives, as consumerism or environmental issues are now mainstream news in the mainstream, in the daily news of the press and in the blogosphere and through social networks. However, fashion industry is also the most profitable industry in the world. From the economic perspective, it can be profitable also as a business.

Although most consumers tend to associate fashion with luxury items and it is often the case that a new product can’t bring the same profit as a product that was successful in the market long time ago, there are a lot of brands and companies out there who, through their clothes, are trying to contribute to their cause, to a sustainable and healthy world. The main problem is that sustainability is not easy to convey to consumers.

prompt: Photosynthesis is a process used by plants and other organisms to
baseline_text:
Photosynthesis is a process used by plants and other organisms to produce their own energy. By making use of energy from sunlight, plants produce carbohydrates and photosynthetic pigments that make plants photosynthetic. In return, animals and plants take and use some plant material in their own food and for fuel.

Photosynthesis occurs in three ways: (1) photosynthesis by the light absorbed by the plants, (2) photosynthesis by algae in small tanks or (3) a simpler process using specialized cell types of algae called algae that are photosynthetic. The photosynthetic cells are specialized for extracting energy from sunlight.

At the most basic level, photosynthesis is done by photosystem II. Photosystem II is the largest and most complex protein complex in all life. It is also the most complex protein complex in the universe. It is the first step in all biological photosynthesis. The light is absorbed to excite light-sensing molecules in photosynthetic proteins. The excitation can send the proteins into reactions leading to the
watermarked_text:
Photosynthesis is a process used by plants and other organisms to use sunlight to generate sugars. The basic steps of the process are seen here:

Step 1. The plant's defense mechanism known as photosynthesis prevents photosynthesis being interrupted. The plant is then free to find a sugar to utilize.

Step 2. The plant's photosynthetic machinery is used to collect more solar energy and convert it into sugars.

Step 3. The sugar the plant collects and converts back to light is the product of sunlight, the plant's sugar, and the food source that the plant will eventually lay bare.

Step 4. The photosynthetic machinery also uses light and carbohydrate molecules for construction and growth, and so it doesn't burn energy during the course of the step.

Step 5. The photosynthetic machinery doesn't produce enough sugars for plants to get enough food to lay bare on their own, so a metabolic chain that converts sugars into the amino acid molecules used for life is necessary -- the amino acid molecules are what plants need
paraphrased_text: Photosynthetic synthesis is a process used by plants and other organisms to use sunlight to generate sugars. Step 1 prevents photosynthesis and the plant is then free to find a sugar to use it. prompt: In a surprise move, the government decided to
baseline_text:
In a surprise move, the government decided to let foreign airlines take over slots at 12 airports, including the two largest, Mumbai and Delhi, after the aviation regulator DGCA issued guidelines on slots in February.

Last month, the Directorate General of Civil Aviation (DGCA) gave Indian airlines the right to take only 15-20 hours' space daily. This was after it revised a three-month-old policy, in which Indian carriers had to wait for at least 14 hours to make a booking of more than 30 minutes.

An industry executive said that the decision will significantly help Air India as it does not have a slot in most of the airports where it operates.

"It will help us a lot. If you look at the schedule of Air India, you will find that this was when we had to depend on the first five hours, when we did not have a slot to book. We used to book from 5am to 5pm. We have slots now of 15-20 hours," a senior official

In a surprise move, the government decided to resume the controversial scheme of reservation under the RTE Act 2014 and this will apply to all states as of April 10 this year, a senior government official said Tuesday.

The ministry, however, expressed the wish that the Maharashtra government’s implementation of the scheme in the state in violation of the provisions of the RTE Act should be considered for re-scrutiny by the government.

The ministry, however, said that the implementation of the scheme by the government in violation of the RTE Act should be considered for further considerations by the government in the case involving the Maharashtra government.

prompt: The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right-angled triangle,
baseline_text:
The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right-angled triangle, the angle at the vertices is always 0. This is true in the absence of any additional bodies, however that's not always true. There's nothing to suggest there are such bodies at all, so you can draw the triangle as a whole (not so much pointwise I assume) rather than as vertices.   But the triangle is still a left-handed triangle, therefore everything else is irrelevant.
So if that’s true, if the triangle is a right handed triangle does that mean the other sides of the triangle can be considered points?
No. Since the triangles are points, they are never points since they don't contain any vertices (or at least there is no way to tell from looking at the triangle) which are the same as each other.  The triangular object just doesn't have any real points to draw from.
But the triangle has a point where the x axis ends so I don’t completely disagree with you just looking at the
watermarked_text:
The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right-angled triangle, if an angle of about 8° about a starting point is added to a starting point, and both angles add a full 180° about the right-hand half of the triangle, that point is the perfect circle whose center is on the right side of the right hand hemisphere
Yes but what I'm trying to figure is if an angle of about 24° about a starting point is added to a starting point how to find the perfect circle whose center is on the center half of the right hand hemisphere
Ok that is easy! A right hand circle has 2 vertices, each vertex has an "angle" and so will each of the other vertices, since the right hand angles all have exactly the same angle, and add the same angle, for any two of the right hand angles, it will add the same angle to the right hand side of the right hand hemisphere  (assuming you have a right hand circle)
Ok so I won't be able to get a perfect circle as a starting

prompt: The future of space exploration will likely involve
baseline_text:
The future of space exploration will likely involve sending people on the space station to get to the moon — but they will probably first launch out to get closer to Jupiter and Saturn.

“With the increasing cost and complexity of mission technologies, the United States needs to reduce our reliance on external launch vehicles to reach out beyond Earth orbit. The use of the space station can provide an important stepping stone for our nation to meet its future space needs,” reads a report from NASA’s National Space Council obtained by National Geographic, adding, “A commercial vehicle will be ready for the 2024 flight phase. If all goes according to plan, a service module could be added to the station in 2025 and a crew could be able to live on board the station from 2024 to 2033.”

Advertisement

The report, prepared by NASA’s National Space Council, includes a host of potential options, including an asteroid-hopping mission, a mission to Mars, and an attempt to grab an asteroid,
watermarked_text:
The future of space exploration will likely involve reusable spacecraft.

Elon Musk is the latest big name to champion reusable vehicles, and he’s also a major proponent of the concept. His company SpaceX is launching reused Falcon 9 rockets now, and he claims that someday it will not be uncommon to see a single vehicle launch five or six rockets per flight.

Such a fleet would allow reusable vehicles to be deployed at locations where one may be unable to launch with the proper rockets for a full mission.

“We want to leave the core in the earth,” Musk explained at the TED2013 conference last month. “There will never be an orbital highway that is sufficient to put everything into orbit. So what do we want to do? We want to get down to an area where there will never be enough rockets. There are places you can deploy multiple missions in space. We can deploy a very long mission. The Earth is essentially unlimited in terms of launches. The Earth has much smaller launch costs so

prompt: Newton's first law of motion states that an object at rest
baseline_text:
Newton's first law of motion states that an object at rest stays at rest, unless acted upon by external causes.    Just a reminder that an object is considered to be at rest if it has absolutely no mass relative to the reference frame or another object.  If something was flying past Newton's first law, like an object in the middle of a big sphere, it would continue to fly.
Does this mean if the body of an object is in a sphere then it doesn't have to stay at a certain point?
For a ball in a sphere, the mass inside the sphere doesn't change relative to center.  So, in practice, yes, it doesn't have to stay at one location.
Right ok that makes sense. Thanks for the help!
Anytime! Always happy to help.  There's a subreddit dedicated to this subject: /r/learnmath/r/askmath  Good luck.  :)
It's my cake day so I need to make sure I post something! Thanks!
Happy
watermarked_text:
Newton's first law of motion states that an object at rest is acting constantly upon another object. When you accelerate any object you must also add a moment of inertia to overcome. I'm confused as to why you would put weight on one side to resist it.
To resist the acceleration, it is simply required that the acceleration is less than initial value at the surface
Right, right. So if you can move up a hill by walking, how would you walk backwards? Same concept. So why would you want to stick weight on the downhill slope? If you accelerate downward then any weight added must be added at an angle to prevent additional momentum being imparted, right? A downward force for you, right? I'm stupid. I understand now.
It's a bit different because of the gradient of the slope, and that is the thing with gravity where your momentum is higher (more momentum) on the upslope. So for a walking object you can really push it down a hill like on the "up" since the momentum is lower.
paraphrased_text: Newton's first law of motion states that an object at rest is constantly acting on another object to overcome. To resist the acceleration it is simply required that acceleration is less than the initial value at the surface? So how would you move down the hill if you accelerated downwards then any added weight must be added at an angle to prevent additional momentum from being imparted. Right? I'm stupid. I understand now. You can really push an object down a hill like the "up" since momentum is lower.

prompt: The psychological effects of social media on teenagers are
baseline_text:
The psychological effects of social media on teenagers are still being researched, but it’s clear that negative experiences with social media and mobile technology in young people have an impact.

Research from the Pew Research Center finds that when teens do not have consistent access to the internet they are more likely to drop out of high school, and to be more likely to experience mental illnesses such as depression and anxiety.

These negative outcomes are all caused by lack of access to media online and digital devices.

Despite the warnings from researchers and the fact that these consequences don’t just affect the teens themselves, mobile devices continue to be widely used as the medium for social communication in the UK and internationally.

So what does this mean for the use of mobile devices in education? What can we do to help the effects of technology on children and youth that we have already observed? And what strategies are being used to counteract these effects?

What are the factors that could possibly be contributing to the negative effects already observed by researchers?
watermarked_text:
The psychological effects of social media on teenagers are just now being revealed, and they’re terrifying.

It turns out regular scrolling through Facebook has enormous effects on teens, and more than half agree that the content they’re seeing on the platform is ruining their lives.

A new independent research study done over a six month period has revealed the effects of social media on teens, especially those in the top 10 percent for most of the time spent on the platform. Seventy-six percent said they feel pressure to keep up with the latest trends because of the addictive nature of the platform and they’re also seeing the effects of the media portrayed on the platform affect other parts of their lives, from sports and music to education and finance.
